## [Ralph] [PLAN] Round 1 | Step: planning
**Time**: 2026-03-26 20:59:05
**Summary**: 

[PLAN]

## Task Understanding

The `_iso8601_datetime_re` regex in `code/src/marshmallow/utils.py` (line 108) is missing a `^` start anchor. While `re.match()` anchors at position 0, the pattern is inconsistent with `_iso8601_date_re` (which has `^`) and would silently accept non-ISO strings if anyone switched to `re.search()`. Two test cases exercise this by passing time-first (`%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d`) and US-style (`%m-%d-%Y %H:%M:%S`) format strings that should be rejected.

## Proposed Approach

Add `^` to the beginning of the `_iso8601_datetime_re` pattern on line 109, changing:
```python
r"(?P<year>\d{4})-(?P<month>\d{1,2})-(?P<day>\d{1,2})"
```
to:
```python
r"^(?P<year>\d{4})-(?P<month>\d{1,2})-(?P<day>\d{1,2})"
```

This is a one-character change. No other files need modification.

## Expected Deliverables

- Single edit to `code/src/marshmallow/utils.py` line 109

## Test Plan

- **Test command:** `docker exec -w /app/code -e PYTHONPATH=src:. <container>_rll python -m pytest tests --tb=short -q`
- **Coverage scope:** All existing datetime deserialization tests plus the two new parameterized test cases for time-first and US-style formats
- **New tests:** 0 — tests are already provided in the test suite; I must not modify test files

## Quality Gate

- No lint/format commands identified in the project config; will rely on test suite passing

requirements.location: src/marshmallow/schema.py — Schema class (currently no from_dict method exists)
requirements.description:
The Schema class is missing a from_dict classmethod. Calling Schema.from_dict(...)
      raises AttributeError: type object 'Schema' has no attribute 'from_dict'. The method
      should dynamically generate a new Schema subclass from a dictionary of field instances,
      without registering it in the class_registry.
requirements.contract:
Signature: classmethod from_dict(fields: dict, name: str = "GeneratedSchema") -> type

      Behavioral expectations:
      - Returns a new class that is a subclass of the calling class (i.e., if called on
        OrderedSchema, the result is a subclass of OrderedSchema, inheriting its Meta options).
      - The returned class has the provided fields dict as its declared fields.
      - The name parameter sets the __name__ attribute of the generated class.
        Default value is "GeneratedSchema".
      - The generated class is NOT registered in class_registry._registry.
        Calling class_registry.get_class("GeneratedSchema") on a generated schema must
        raise RegistryError.
      - The generated schema is fully functional: it can be instantiated, dump, and load
        data using its declared fields and any Meta options inherited from the calling class.

requirements.description:
The Field.fail() method raises a ValidationError but does not emit any deprecation
      warning. Tests expect a DeprecationWarning to be emitted when fail() is called,
      indicating it is deprecated in favor of make_error(). Currently, pytest.warns(
      DeprecationWarning) fails because no warning is emitted.
requirements.contract:
Signature: fail(self, key: str, **kwargs) -> None  (raises ValidationError)

      Behavioral expectations:
      - Before raising the ValidationError, call warnings.warn() to emit a DeprecationWarning.
      - The warning message should indicate that fail() is deprecated (e.g., direct callers
        should use make_error() instead).
      - After emitting the warning, the method must still raise the ValidationError produced
        by self.make_error(key=key, **kwargs), preserving existing exception behavior.
      - The stacklevel for warnings.warn should be set appropriately so callers see the
        warning at the call site, not inside fields.py itself (stacklevel=2 is typical).
requirements.acceptance_tests:
tests/test_fields.py::TestErrorMessages::test_fail[custom-Custom error message.]
      tests/test_fields.py::TestErrorMessages::test_fail[null-Field may not be null.]
      tests/test_fields.py::TestErrorMessages::test_fail[required-Missing data for required field.]
      tests/test_fields.py::TestErrorMessages::test_fail[validator_failed-Invalid value.]

requirements.location:
src/marshmallow/fields.py — Nested class, schema property (around line 442)
      and __init__ (around line 426)
requirements.description:
When a Schema instance (rather than a class) is passed to fields.Nested, the schema
      property returns it directly without applying the only/exclude parameters from the
      Nested field. Additionally, self.many is set only from the 'many' kwarg and does not
      fall back to the schema instance's many attribute. This causes three observable failures:

      1. fields.Nested(ArtistSchema(), only=("last",)): the only restriction is silently
         ignored; both "first" and "last" fields are serialized.
      2. fields.Nested(BookSchema(many=True)): self.many is False, so schema.dump() is
         called with many=False, overriding the instance's many=True setting and producing
         an empty dict instead of a list.
      3. fields.List(fields.Nested(Child(only=("name", "age")))): when the parent schema
         has only=("children.age",), the intersection with the nested schema instance's
         only is not applied — the nested schema keeps its original only=("name", "age").
requirements.contract:
In the schema property:
      - When self.nested is a SchemaABC instance, the returned schema instance must have
        its only and exclude attributes updated to reflect self.only and self.exclude
        respectively. These attributes on the Nested field are set by the parent schema's
        only/exclude resolution (via List._bind_to_schema or directly). The update must
        happen on each property access to support multiple instantiations of the parent schema.

      In __init__ or the schema property:
      - When self.nested is a SchemaABC instance and the 'many' kwarg was not explicitly
        passed (i.e., self.many defaults to False), self.many should be set to the schema
        instance's many attribute instead. This ensures dump() is called with the correct
        many value.

      In _serialize:
      - schema.dump(nested_obj, many=self.many) should use the correctly resolved self.many.

      Boundary conditions:
      - An explicit many=True kwarg on fields.Nested must take precedence over the schema
        instance's many attribute.
      - When only/exclude on the Nested field is empty/None (i.e., no restriction from
        parent), the schema instance's own only/exclude must be preserved unchanged.
      - Multiple calls to dump() on the same parent schema must each correctly apply the
        only/exclude constraints (no state leakage between instantiations).

requirements.location:
src/marshmallow/fields.py — Url.__init__ (around line 1461) and
      Email.__init__ (around line 1489)
requirements.description:
When a tuple is passed as the validate argument to fields.Url or fields.Email,
      Field.__init__ stores it as-is (a tuple). Url.__init__ and Email.__init__ then call
      self.validators.insert(0, ...) which fails with:
        AttributeError: 'tuple' object has no attribute 'insert'
      This prevents users from passing a tuple of validators to URL/Email fields, even
      though Field.__init__ accepts any iterable for the validate parameter.
requirements.contract:
- In Url.__init__ and Email.__init__, before calling self.validators.insert(0, ...),
        self.validators must be a mutable list.
      - If self.validators is not a list at that point (e.g., it is a tuple), it should be
        converted to a list first.
      - The URL/email validator must still be prepended at index 0 of the validators list.
      - All other validators originally passed by the user must be preserved in order.
      - Alternatively, Field.__init__ may be modified to always store validators as a list
        (rather than preserving the original iterable type), as long as URL/Email insertion
        behavior is unaffected.

requirements.description:
DateTime._bind_to_schema accesses schema.opts to read the datetimeformat (or
      dateformat for Date subclass) Meta option. When a DateTime or Date field is nested
      inside a container field such as Tuple, _bind_to_schema receives the Tuple field
      (not the Schema) as its schema argument. Tuple fields have no opts attribute,
      causing:
        AttributeError: 'Tuple' object has no attribute 'opts'
      This prevents using DateTime or Date fields as elements of a Tuple field inside
      a Schema that declares datetimeformat or dateformat in its Meta class.
requirements.contract:
- DateTime._bind_to_schema must not unconditionally access schema.opts when schema
        may be a container field rather than a Schema instance.
      - The method should resolve the opts from the root schema (reachable via self.root,
        which traverses the parent chain to find the nearest SchemaABC ancestor).
      - If self.root is None (field not yet bound to a schema) or the root has no opts,
        the format should fall back to self.DEFAULT_FORMAT.
      - This fix must cover both DateTime and its subclass Date, since Date inherits
        _bind_to_schema from DateTime and uses SCHEMA_OPTS_VAR_NAME = "dateformat".
      - Fields inside both List and Tuple containers must correctly inherit the format
        from their root schema's Meta options.

      Accepted behavioral contract:
        Given a Schema with class Meta: datetimeformat = "iso8601", a field
        foo = List(DateTime()) must produce foo.inner.format == "iso8601", and
        bar = Tuple((DateTime(),)) must produce bar.tuple_fields[0].format == "iso8601"
        after schema instantiation.

requirements.description:
The `_iso8601_time_re` compiled regular expression is missing the `$` end-of-string anchor.
      Both sibling patterns (`_iso8601_datetime_re` and `_iso8601_date_re`) include a trailing `$`,
      but `_iso8601_time_re` does not. Because `re.match()` only anchors at the start of a string,
      the absence of `$` allows `_iso8601_time_re` to match any string whose prefix is a valid
      ISO 8601 time, even if substantial trailing content follows. For example,
      `"22:19:16 2026-03-26"` (a time string followed by a date) would match and be silently
      accepted instead of being rejected as an invalid time value.
requirements.contract:
`_iso8601_time_re` must fully anchor the pattern to the end of the string so that
      `from_iso_time(value)` raises `ValueError` for any `value` that contains characters
      beyond what constitutes a well-formed ISO 8601 time string
      (HH:MM[:SS[.ffffff]]). Strings such as `"22:19:16 2026-03-26"` or
      `"10:11:12 extra"` must not be accepted as valid times.

      The required behavioral contract for `from_iso_time`:
      - Input: a string value.
      - Accepted output: a `datetime.time` object when the ENTIRE string matches the
        pattern `HH:MM[:SS[.ffffff]]` (no leading or trailing characters outside this format).
      - Rejected (raises `ValueError`): any string that contains characters before or
        after the time component, including date fragments, whitespace, or other text.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
1. `utils.from_iso_time("22:19:16 2026-03-26")` must raise `ValueError`
         (currently it silently succeeds by matching only the `"22:19:16"` prefix).
      2. `utils.from_iso_time("10:11:12")` must still return `datetime.time(10, 11, 12)`.
      3. `utils.from_iso_time("10:11:12.123456")` must still return
         `datetime.time(10, 11, 12, 123456)`.
      4. `fields.Time().deserialize("badvalue")` must still raise `ValidationError`
         ("Not a valid time."), confirming no regression in existing tests:
         `tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_time_field_deserialization`.
      5. The two non-passed tests that exercise `fields.DateTime()` with strings in
         `%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d` and `%m-%d-%Y %H:%M:%S` formats must raise
         `ValidationError("Not a valid datetime.")`:
         - `tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[<HH:MM:SS YYYY-MM-DD>]`
         - `tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[<MM-DD-YYYY HH:MM:SS>]`
         (where the bracketed IDs reflect the current-time parametrize values generated
         by `dt.datetime.now().strftime(...)` at test-collection time).

requirements.location:
code/src/marshmallow/utils.py — module-level regex constant `_iso8601_datetime_re`
      and function `from_iso_datetime`
requirements.description:
The `_iso8601_datetime_re` regular expression is used by `from_iso_datetime` to
      validate and parse ISO 8601 datetime strings. Although `re.match` implicitly anchors
      the match at the start of the string, the regex pattern lacks an explicit start anchor
      (`^`) and, more importantly, must ensure that strings whose structure matches a
      non-ISO ordering of date/time components (e.g., time-before-date:
      `"HH:MM:SS YYYY-MM-DD"`, or a US-style date: `"MM-DD-YYYY HH:MM:SS"`) are
      categorically rejected.

      Currently, the test suite parametrizes `test_invalid_datetime_deserialization` with
      two dynamically-generated values:
        - `dt.datetime.now().strftime("%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d")`  → e.g. `"22:19:16 2026-03-26"`
        - `dt.datetime.now().strftime("%m-%d-%Y %H:%M:%S")` → e.g. `"03-26-2026 22:19:16"`

      These values are NOT valid ISO 8601 datetimes and the `DateTime` field (using the
      default `"iso"` format) MUST raise `ValidationError` for them. The behavioral
      contract of `from_iso_datetime` must be clear and unambiguous: it SHALL accept only
      strings whose date component appears before the time component in YYYY-MM-DD order,
      separated by `T` or a single space, matching the ISO 8601 canonical form.
requirements.contract:
`from_iso_datetime(value: str) -> datetime.datetime`

      - Input: a string value.
      - Accepted formats: strings that match the ISO 8601 datetime pattern
        `YYYY-MM-DD[T ]HH:MM[:SS[.ffffff]][Z|±HH:MM]`.
        The year component MUST appear first and consist of exactly 4 digits followed by
        a `-` separator; the month follows (1–2 digits), then another `-`, then the day
        (1–2 digits), then a `T` or single space separator, then hours, minutes, and
        optional seconds/microseconds/timezone.
      - Rejected formats: ANY string that does not start with `YYYY-MM-DD` (4-digit year,
        then `-`, then month, then `-`, then day). This explicitly includes:
          * Time-before-date strings such as `"HH:MM:SS YYYY-MM-DD"`.
          * Non-ISO date-order strings such as `"MM-DD-YYYY HH:MM:SS"`.
          * Strings lacking the time component entirely (e.g. `"2018-01-01"`).
          * Non-string inputs (int, list, etc.).
      - On rejection: raise `ValueError` (which `DateTime._deserialize` catches and
        converts to a `ValidationError` with message matching "Not a valid datetime.").
      - The regex `_iso8601_datetime_re` MUST NOT match any of the rejected formats via
        either `re.match` or `re.search`. Adding an explicit `^` anchor is recommended to
        document intent clearly.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
All eight parametrized variants of
        `tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization`
      must pass, including the two dynamically-generated ones:
        - `test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[<HH:MM:SS YYYY-MM-DD>]`
          (the value of `dt.datetime.now().strftime("%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d")` at collection time)
        - `test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[<MM-DD-YYYY HH:MM:SS>]`
          (the value of `dt.datetime.now().strftime("%m-%d-%Y %H:%M:%S")` at collection time)

      Each must raise `ValidationError` whose string representation matches the pattern
      `"Not a valid datetime."`.

      Additionally:
        - `test_custom_date_format_datetime_field_deserialization` must continue to pass,
          confirming that custom-format deserialization paths are unaffected.
        - All other existing tests in `test_deserialization.py` must continue to pass
          (no regression).

requirements.location:
code/src/marshmallow/utils.py — module-level regex constant `_iso8601_datetime_re`
      and function `from_iso_datetime`
requirements.description:
The `_iso8601_datetime_re` regular expression is used by `from_iso_datetime` to
      validate and parse ISO8601 datetime strings. The regex must strictly reject any
      string that does not begin with a four-digit year followed by a hyphen-separated
      month and day (i.e., `YYYY-MM-DD`). Currently, datetime strings in which the
      time component appears before the date component — such as those produced by
      `strftime("%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d")` (e.g. `"22:19:16 2026-03-26"`) — or strings
      in which the date components are in a non-ISO order — such as those produced by
      `strftime("%m-%d-%Y %H:%M:%S")` (e.g. `"03-26-2026 22:19:16"`) — must NOT be
      accepted as valid ISO8601 datetime strings.

      The function `from_iso_datetime` is expected to raise `ValueError` for any input
      that does not fully conform to the ISO8601 datetime pattern
      `YYYY-MM-DD[T|space]HH:MM[:SS[.ffffff]][timezone]`. Strings where the time
      portion precedes the date portion, or where the date is expressed in a
      non-year-first order, must all be rejected.
requirements.contract:
Inputs and outputs of `from_iso_datetime(value: str) -> datetime.datetime`:
      - Accepts: strings strictly conforming to ISO8601 format where the date portion
        begins with a four-digit year, e.g.:
          * `"2026-03-26T22:19:16"`
          * `"2026-03-26 22:19:16"`
          * `"2026-03-26T22:19:16.123456+05:30"`
      - Rejects with `ValueError`: any string that does not match the ISO8601 pattern
        anchored at the start with `\d{4}-`, including:
          * Strings where time comes before date: `"22:19:16 2026-03-26"`
            (format `%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d`)
          * Strings where date is in month-day-year order: `"03-26-2026 22:19:16"`
            (format `%m-%d-%Y %H:%M:%S`)
          * Strings that are date-only without a time component: `"2026-03-26"`
          * Strings that are year-only: `"2026"`
          * Non-string types must also cause an error (TypeError or ValueError)

      The regex `_iso8601_datetime_re` must:
        1. Be anchored at the start (`^`) with `(?P<year>\d{4})-` ensuring exactly
           four digits precede the first hyphen.
        2. Be anchored at the end (`$`) to prevent partial matches.
        3. Require both date and time portions — the separator `[T ]` (T or single
           space) must be present and the time part `HH:MM` must follow the date.
        4. Return `None` for `re.match(...)` when either of the above non-conforming
           formats is tested against it.

      `from_iso_datetime` must raise `ValueError` (not return a fallback value) when
      the regex match returns `None`.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
The following test cases in the parametrized test must pass:

        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[<HH:MM:SS YYYY-MM-DD string>]
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[<MM-DD-YYYY HH:MM:SS string>]

      Specifically, for any value produced at test-collection time by:
        - `dt.datetime.now().strftime("%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d")`
        - `dt.datetime.now().strftime("%m-%d-%Y %H:%M:%S")`

      calling `fields.DateTime().deserialize(value)` must raise
      `marshmallow.exceptions.ValidationError` whose string representation matches
      the pattern `"Not a valid datetime."`.

      Additionally, all previously passing parametrize cases must continue to pass:
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[not-a-datetime]
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[42]
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[]
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[in_value3]
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[2018]
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[2018-01-01]

requirements.location:
code/src/marshmallow/utils.py — module-level regex constant `_iso8601_datetime_re`
      and function `from_iso_datetime(value)` (lines 108–155)
requirements.description:
The `_iso8601_datetime_re` regular expression and `from_iso_datetime` function must
      strictly enforce ISO 8601 ordering and structure for datetime strings.  In the
      current codebase the regex anchors on `^…$` and requires `YYYY-MM-DD` first, but
      the test parametrize list has been extended with two dynamically-generated values
      that expose edge-case handling:

        1. Time-before-date format: `dt.datetime.now().strftime("%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d")`
           e.g. `"22:19:16 2026-03-26"` — a `HH:MM:SS YYYY-MM-DD` layout.
        2. US-style date format: `dt.datetime.now().strftime("%m-%d-%Y %H:%M:%S")`
           e.g. `"03-26-2026 22:19:16"` — a `MM-DD-YYYY HH:MM:SS` layout.

      These strings must NOT be accepted as valid ISO 8601 datetimes.  If the regex or
      the function inadvertently matches or does not raise an exception for these inputs,
      the upstream `DateTime._deserialize` will return a datetime object instead of
      raising `ValidationError`, causing `test_invalid_datetime_deserialization` to fail.

      Additionally, the `_iso8601_date_re` regex (line 115) currently lacks a `^` start
      anchor, making it subtly inconsistent with `_iso8601_datetime_re`; while `re.match`
      implicitly anchors at position 0, the missing anchor is a latent correctness risk.
requirements.contract:
• `from_iso_datetime(value: str) -> datetime.datetime`
        - Accepts only strings that conform to ISO 8601 datetime format:
          `YYYY-MM-DD[T| ]HH:MM[:SS[.ffffff]][Z|±HH[:MM]]`
          where the date component (`YYYY-MM-DD`) always precedes the time component.
        - Must raise `ValueError` for any string that does not fully match this layout,
          including (but not limited to):
            * strings where the time component appears before the date (e.g. `HH:MM:SS YYYY-MM-DD`)
            * strings with non-ISO date orderings (e.g. `MM-DD-YYYY`)
            * partial strings such as bare years (`"2018"`) or bare dates (`"2018-01-01"`)
        - The function must use a regex that is anchored at both `^` (start) and `$` (end)
          so that no substring of a longer, invalid string can satisfy the pattern.
        - The year group must require exactly four digits (`\d{4}`).

      • `_iso8601_date_re` regex
        - Must include a `^` start anchor so it is consistent with `_iso8601_datetime_re`
          and unambiguously rejects non-ISO date strings when used with `re.match` or
          `re.fullmatch`.

      • No change to the public API signature of `from_iso_datetime` or `from_iso_date`.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
All parametrize cases of:
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization

      must pass, specifically including:
        - test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[<HH:MM:SS YYYY-MM-DD>]
          (value produced by `dt.datetime.now().strftime("%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d")`)
        - test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[<MM-DD-YYYY HH:MM:SS>]
          (value produced by `dt.datetime.now().strftime("%m-%d-%Y %H:%M:%S")`)
        - All pre-existing parametrize cases (not-a-datetime, 42, "", [], "2018",
          "2018-01-01") must continue to pass.

      The following must also continue to pass without regression:
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_custom_date_format_datetime_field_deserialization
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_datetime_deserialization
        tests/test_utils.py  (all utils-level datetime/date parsing tests)

requirements.description:
The `_iso8601_datetime_re` regular expression currently includes a redundant `^`
      start-of-string anchor (e.g., `r"^(?P<year>\d{4})-..."`) even though the function
      `from_iso_datetime` uses `re.match()`, which already implicitly anchors at the
      start. More importantly, the regex does NOT enforce a start-of-string anchor
      that prevents partial matches when the function is used or reused with
      `re.search()`. The target version removes the explicit `^` from the first
      concatenated string of `_iso8601_datetime_re` while keeping the `$` end anchor,
      making the anchoring intent explicit only via the `$` terminator and the
      `match()` call site.

      The functional contract being tested is: `from_iso_datetime` must reject any
      string that is not a valid ISO 8601 datetime. Specifically, strings in which the
      time component precedes the date (e.g., `"HH:MM:SS YYYY-MM-DD"`) or in which
      the date uses non-ISO field ordering (e.g., `"MM-DD-YYYY HH:MM:SS"`) must cause
      `from_iso_datetime` to raise `ValueError`, which `DateTime._deserialize` must
      then convert to a `ValidationError` with message `"Not a valid datetime."`.

      The current behaviour is correct for these two formats because `match()` already
      anchors at the start, but the explicit `^` is inconsistent with the target
      codebase and creates maintenance confusion about where anchoring is enforced.
requirements.contract:
`from_iso_datetime(value: str) -> datetime.datetime`

      - Accepts only strings that fully match ISO 8601 extended datetime format:
        `YYYY-MM-DD[T|space]HH:MM[:SS[.microseconds]][Z|±HH:MM]`
      - The match must be anchored at the END of the string (via `$`), preventing
        acceptance of strings with trailing garbage.
      - Strings where the time component appears before the date component
        (e.g., `"22:19:16 2026-03-26"`) must NOT match and must raise `ValueError`.
      - Strings using non-ISO date field ordering (e.g., `"03-26-2026 22:19:16"`)
        must NOT match and must raise `ValueError`.
      - Strings containing only a date part (e.g., `"2018-01-01"`) without a time
        component must NOT match and must raise `ValueError`.
      - The `_iso8601_datetime_re` pattern should NOT include a redundant `^` anchor
        in the compiled pattern string (anchoring is handled by using `re.match()`
        at the call site).
requirements.acceptance_tests:
- `tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization`
        with parameterized values matching `dt.datetime.now().strftime("%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d")`
        and `dt.datetime.now().strftime("%m-%d-%Y %H:%M:%S")` — both must raise
        `ValidationError` with message matching `"Not a valid datetime."`.
      - Existing parametrized cases (`"not-a-datetime"`, `42`, `""`, `[]`, `"2018"`,
        `"2018-01-01"`) must continue to raise `ValidationError`.
      - `tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_custom_date_format_datetime_field_deserialization`
        must continue to pass (valid ISO strings must still deserialize correctly).

requirements.location:
code/src/marshmallow/fields.py — `DateTime._deserialize` method (approximately
      line 1126 in current code)
requirements.description:
In `DateTime._deserialize`, the falsy-value guard uses the deprecated
      `self.fail("invalid", input=value, obj_type=self.OBJ_TYPE)` call. In the
      target codebase this is updated to use
      `raise self.make_error("invalid", input=value, obj_type=self.OBJ_TYPE)` directly,
      which is the non-deprecated, explicit API. The deprecated `self.fail()` path
      issues a `DeprecationWarning`, which may surface as test noise and masks
      intentional raise semantics.
requirements.contract:
`DateTime._deserialize(self, value, attr, data, **kwargs) -> datetime.datetime`

      - When `value` is falsy (empty string, empty list, zero, etc.), the method must
        raise `ValidationError` via `raise self.make_error("invalid", input=value,
        obj_type=self.OBJ_TYPE)`.
      - The raised `ValidationError` message must match the pattern
        `"Not a valid datetime."` (where `obj_type` is `"datetime"`).
      - No deprecation warnings should be emitted during deserialization.
      - All other branches (non-falsy invalid values, valid values) must be unaffected.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
- `tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization`
        with values `""` and `[]` (falsy inputs) must raise `ValidationError` with
        message matching `"Not a valid datetime."` without any deprecation warning.
      - `tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_field_toggle_show_invalid_value_in_error_message`
        must continue to pass (custom error message formatting).

requirements.description:
The compiled regular expression `_iso8601_datetime_re` is missing a `^` anchor at the
      start of its pattern. This is an inconsistency with `_iso8601_date_re` (line 115), which
      explicitly anchors both start (`^`) and end (`$`). While the current call site uses
      `re.match()` — which implicitly anchors at position 0 — the absence of `^` in the pattern
      itself leaves the regex semantically underspecified. More importantly, if the matching
      strategy is ever changed to `re.search()` or `re.fullmatch()`, the behaviour would silently
      diverge: `re.search()` would find ISO-like substrings inside otherwise-invalid strings
      (e.g. "2026-03-26" embedded at the end of "22:19:16 2026-03-26"), causing clearly invalid
      datetime strings to be accepted rather than raising a `ValueError`.

      The practical effect is that datetime strings in non-ISO orderings — such as
      `"%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d"` (time before date) or `"%m-%d-%Y %H:%M:%S"` (US date format) —
      must be definitively rejected.  The regex must enforce that any valid ISO 8601 datetime
      string must begin with a four-digit year component.
requirements.contract:
The `_iso8601_datetime_re` pattern must:
      - Begin with an explicit `^` anchor so the pattern is self-contained and does not rely
        solely on the call site using `re.match()`.
      - Continue to end with the existing `$` anchor, fully constraining the match to the entire
        input string.
      - Retain all existing named capture groups and optional components unchanged
        (year, month, day, hour, minute, second, microsecond, tzinfo).
      - Accept all currently-valid ISO 8601 datetime strings (e.g., "2026-03-26T22:19:16",
        "2026-03-26 22:19:16", "2026-3-26T22:19:16+05:30", "2026-03-26T22:19:16.123456Z").
      - Reject any string that does not begin with a four-digit year followed by `-`, including:
        * Strings in `%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d` order (e.g., "22:19:16 2026-03-26")
        * Strings in `%m-%d-%Y %H:%M:%S` order (e.g., "03-26-2026 22:19:16")
        * Date-only strings such as "2026-03-26" (no time component)
        * Year-only strings such as "2018"

      When `_iso8601_datetime_re` does not match, `from_iso_datetime` must raise `ValueError`.
      `DateTime._deserialize` must convert that `ValueError` into a `ValidationError` whose
      message matches `"Not a valid datetime."`.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
All eight parametrized cases of
      `tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization`
      must pass, specifically including:
      - The case whose `in_value` is `dt.datetime.now().strftime("%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d")`
        (a string in time-before-date order, e.g., "22:19:16 2026-03-26").
      - The case whose `in_value` is `dt.datetime.now().strftime("%m-%d-%Y %H:%M:%S")`
        (a string in US date-with-time order, e.g., "03-26-2026 22:19:16").

      Additionally, the following regression tests must continue to pass:
      - `tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_datetime_deserialization`
      - `tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_custom_date_format_datetime_field_deserialization`
      - All other tests in `tests/test_deserialization.py` that involve `DateTime` fields.

requirements.location:
code/src/marshmallow/utils.py — function `from_iso_datetime` and the module-level
      regex `_iso8601_datetime_re`
requirements.description:
The `from_iso_datetime` function is responsible for parsing ISO 8601 datetime strings
      for the default `DateTime` field.  The two non-passed test cases use dynamically-
      generated strings that carry a non-ISO ordering:

        • `dt.datetime.now().strftime("%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d")` → e.g. "22:19:16 2026-03-26"
          (time component BEFORE date component)
        • `dt.datetime.now().strftime("%m-%d-%Y %H:%M:%S")` → e.g. "03-26-2026 22:19:16"
          (US month-day-year ordering, not ISO year-month-day)

      Both strings must be treated as invalid by `fields.DateTime()` (used with no explicit
      `format` argument, i.e. the default ISO deserialization path).  The test asserts that
      calling `field.deserialize(value)` raises `ValidationError` with the message
      "Not a valid datetime." for each of these inputs.

      The root cause is that the ISO 8601 validation must be strict: the year-month-day
      ordering must appear at the start of the string, and neither a time-first layout nor
      a non-ISO date ordering may be silently accepted.  Any parsing helper used internally
      (regex, stdlib, or third-party) must enforce this strictness and raise `ValueError`
      (or an equivalent) for non-conforming strings so that `DateTime._deserialize` can
      catch it and surface `ValidationError`.
requirements.contract:
Behavioural contract for `from_iso_datetime(value: str) -> datetime.datetime`:

      1. **Accepted format** — The function must accept strings that strictly match the
         ISO 8601 combined date-time pattern:

             YYYY-MM-DD(T| )HH:MM[:SS[.ffffff]][Z|±HH[:MM]]

         where:
         - The date part (YYYY-MM-DD) MUST appear before the time part.
         - The year field MUST be exactly 4 digits.
         - The separator between date and time MUST be either `T` or a single space.

      2. **Rejected formats — must raise `ValueError`** — Any string that does NOT match
         the above layout must cause a `ValueError` to be raised.  This explicitly includes:
         - Time-first strings such as "HH:MM:SS YYYY-MM-DD"
         - US-style date strings such as "MM-DD-YYYY HH:MM:SS"
         - Date-only strings such as "YYYY-MM-DD" (no time component)
         - Year-only strings such as "YYYY"
         - Arbitrary non-datetime strings
         - Non-string types (already handled upstream, but must not produce silent success)

      3. **`fields.DateTime._deserialize` contract** — When `self.format` is `None` or
         resolves to `"iso"` / `"iso8601"`, any call to `field.deserialize(value)` with a
         value that does not conform to the ISO 8601 date-time format (point 2 above) MUST
         raise `marshmallow.exceptions.ValidationError` whose message matches the pattern
         "Not a valid datetime."

      4. The function signature and return type must remain unchanged:
         `from_iso_datetime(value: str) -> datetime.datetime`
requirements.acceptance_tests:
The following parametrized test cases (both currently non-passed) must pass:

        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[<HH:MM:SS YYYY-MM-DD>]
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[<MM-DD-YYYY HH:MM:SS>]

      where the bracketed value is the string produced by `dt.datetime.now()` at collection
      time (the exact timestamp will vary between runs).

      Additionally, the following already-passing parametrized cases must continue to pass
      (regression guard):

        test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[not-a-datetime]
        test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[42]
        test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[]
        test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[in_value3]   (i.e. [])
        test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[2018]
        test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[2018-01-01]

      The broader deserialization and serialization test suites must remain green:

        tests/test_deserialization.py
        tests/test_serialization.py

requirements.description:
The compiled regular expression `_iso8601_datetime_re` begins with a `^` anchor (line 109).
      Because every call site uses `re.Pattern.match()` (which implicitly anchors to the start of the
      string), the explicit `^` is redundant. Its presence creates a subtle inconsistency: the
      pattern cannot be reused with `re.search()` in the future without unintended behavior, and it
      differs from the expected (target) implementation. This mismatch causes the pattern to behave
      slightly differently from the canonical form expected by the test suite.
requirements.contract:
Remove the `^` anchor from the beginning of `_iso8601_datetime_re`. The pattern must still:
      - Match ISO 8601 datetime strings of the form YYYY-MM-DD[T ]HH:MM[:SS[.ffffff]][Z|+HH:MM]
        when applied via `re.match()` or `re.search()`.
      - Reject strings that do not start with a four-digit year followed by `-month-day`.
      - Continue to accept both `T` and space as the date-time separator.
      - Continue to capture named groups: year, month, day, hour, minute, second, microsecond,
        tzinfo.
      The function `from_iso_datetime(value)` must retain its contract: parse an ISO 8601 datetime
      string and return a `datetime.datetime`, raising `ValueError` for any non-conforming input.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
- `tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization`
        (all parametrized cases, including those whose IDs include strings of format
        `%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d` and `%m-%d-%Y %H:%M:%S`) must raise
        `ValidationError` matching "Not a valid datetime."
      - `tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_iso_datetime_field_deserialization`
        must continue to pass for all parametrized (fmt, value, expected, aware) combinations,
        including timezone-aware strings such as "2013-11-10T01:23:45+00:00".
      - `tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_custom_date_format_datetime_field_deserialization`
        must continue to pass.

requirements.description:
The compiled regular expression `_iso8601_time_re` currently ends with a `$` anchor appended
      directly after the optional seconds/microseconds group (line 119):

        r"(?::(?P<second>\d{1,2})(?:\.(?P<microsecond>\d{1,6})\d{0,6})?)?$"

      Because `re.Pattern.match()` is used at every call site, this `$` forces the pattern to
      match only when the entire input string is consumed after the optional seconds group. This
      means that a string such as "22:19:16 extra-text" would be rejected even though its time
      prefix "22:19:16" is a perfectly valid ISO 8601 time. The `$` here is extraneous and
      inconsistent with the target implementation, which omits it so that `re.match()` matches
      only the leading time portion of the input.
requirements.contract:
Remove the trailing `$` from `_iso8601_time_re`. The updated pattern must:
      - Match any string whose prefix conforms to ISO 8601 time: HH:MM[:SS[.ffffff]] when
        applied via `re.match()`.
      - Not require the entire input string to be consumed; trailing characters after a valid
        time prefix are permitted.
      - Continue to capture named groups: hour, minute, second, microsecond.
      The function `from_iso_time(value)` retains its public contract: parse an ISO 8601 time
      string and return a `datetime.time`, raising `ValueError` for strings that do not begin
      with a recognisable HH:MM pattern.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
- `tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_time_field_deserialization`
        must continue to pass for all parametrized cases (["badvalue", "", [], 42]).
      - `tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization`
        parametrized cases whose `in_value` is generated by
        `dt.datetime.now().strftime("%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d")` must raise `ValidationError` matching
        "Not a valid datetime." (i.e., the `DateTime` field must still reject time-first strings
        irrespective of any change to the time regex).
      - Any existing time-deserialization tests (e.g.,
        `tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization`) that validate correct
        `datetime.time` objects are returned for valid ISO 8601 time strings must continue to pass.

requirements.description:
The `_iso8601_datetime_re` compiled regular expression is missing a `^` start
      anchor. All other ISO 8601 regexes in the same file (`_iso8601_date_re`,
      `_iso8601_time_re`) are either used with explicit `^` in their pattern or rely
      solely on `re.match()`. For `_iso8601_datetime_re`, the pattern ends with `$`
      but does not begin with `^`, making it incomplete as a full-string matcher and
      inconsistent with the rest of the module. This means the regex does not
      explicitly constrain the match to start at position 0 of the string; it
      depends solely on the caller using `re.match()` rather than `re.search()`.
      As a result, any future refactoring that switches to `re.search()` (or any
      other non-anchored search) would silently allow non-ISO datetime strings —
      such as strings in `"%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d"` or `"%m-%d-%Y %H:%M:%S"` formats —
      to be accepted as valid ISO 8601 datetimes. The two non-passed test cases
      exercise exactly these format strings and require that `fields.DateTime()`
      (using the default ISO format) raises `ValidationError` for them.
requirements.contract:
The `_iso8601_datetime_re` pattern MUST begin with `^` so that it is a
      fully-anchored pattern (both `^` at the start and `$` at the end), consistent
      with `_iso8601_date_re`. The behaviour of `from_iso_datetime(value)` must
      remain:
      - Returns a `datetime.datetime` object for any valid ISO 8601 datetime string
        (with `T` or space separator, optional seconds, optional microseconds,
        optional timezone offset in `Z` / `+HH:MM` / `-HH:MM` forms).
      - Raises `ValueError` for any string that does not conform to ISO 8601
        datetime format, including (but not limited to):
          * Pure date strings such as `"2018-01-01"`
          * Pure year strings such as `"2018"`
          * Strings where the time component appears before the date component,
            e.g. `"22:19:16 2026-03-26"` (format `%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d`)
          * Strings in US-style date-then-time order,
            e.g. `"03-26-2026 22:19:16"` (format `%m-%d-%Y %H:%M:%S`)
          * Non-string values (these raise `TypeError` before the regex is reached)
          * Falsy values such as `""` or `[]` (handled upstream in `_deserialize`)
      The `from_iso_datetime` function signature and return type must not change.
      Callers that currently rely on `ValidationError` being raised for invalid
      inputs via `fields.DateTime._deserialize` must continue to receive
      `ValidationError` with message matching `"Not a valid datetime."`.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
Primary test cases (must all pass):
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[not-a-datetime]
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[42]
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[]
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[2018]
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[2018-01-01]
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[<HH:MM:SS YYYY-MM-DD value generated by dt.datetime.now()>]
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_invalid_datetime_deserialization[<MM-DD-YYYY HH:MM:SS value generated by dt.datetime.now()>]

      The two dynamically-parameterised cases above correspond to:
        dt.datetime.now().strftime("%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d")   — time-first format
        dt.datetime.now().strftime("%m-%d-%Y %H:%M:%S")   — US date + time format

      Both MUST raise `marshmallow.exceptions.ValidationError` with a message
      matching "Not a valid datetime." when passed to `fields.DateTime().deserialize()`.

      Regression tests that must continue to pass:
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_custom_date_format_datetime_field_deserialization
        tests/test_deserialization.py::TestFieldDeserialization::test_datetime_field_deserialization
        (and all other datetime-related deserialization tests in the suite)

      Custom verification criterion:
        Calling `utils.from_iso_datetime(dt.datetime.now().strftime("%H:%M:%S %Y-%m-%d"))`
        must raise `ValueError`.
        Calling `utils.from_iso_datetime(dt.datetime.now().strftime("%m-%d-%Y %H:%M:%S"))`
        must raise `ValueError`.
